name: Rocky Range
short_description: BLM public land near Beaver offering excellent rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony. Free access with daily collection limits makes this ideal for Utah gem hunters.
description:
Rocky Range sits within Utah's Basin and Range physiographic province, a region characterized by tilted fault-block mountains and broad valleys. This BLM land provides excellent public rockhounding opportunities without fees or permits, following standard Utah recreational collection rules. The area is known for colorful agate nodules and jasper specimens that weather out of hillside slopes and wash beds, particularly in the lower elevations surrounding the range.

The geology here reflects Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary deposition, with silica-rich horizons that crystallized into the agate and chalcedony found weathering from outcrop exposures and scattered across desert surfaces. Collectors report finding quality specimens in washes after seasonal runoff and on exposed hillside slopes where erosion continuously exposes fresh material.

Expect semi-arid sagebrush terrain with scattered rock outcrops and seasonal wash drainage. Elevation ranges from roughly 5,500 to 6,500 feet. Rockhounding is best done by hiking gentle slopes and examining wash bottoms, where specimens accumulate naturally. The area is popular with local collectors and remains relatively accessible compared to more remote Utah rockhounding sites.

Always pack water, sun protection, and a map. Cell service is spotty, so carry a GPS device or download offline maps. The distance from the nearest town of Beaver makes this a day trip destination, with roughly 15-20 miles of driving required from town.
history: Rocky Range lies within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-era volcanic activity and associated hydrothermal systems deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into agate and chalcedony nodules. The exposures visible today reflect millions of years of erosion that continuously reveal fresh specimens in washes and hillside outcrops.
tips: Search wash bottoms and arroyo beds after seasonal rain, where water has concentrated specimens. Look for translucent to opaque agate showing banding, fortification patterns, or fire colors; specimens typically range from marble to fist-sized. Examine south and west-facing hillside slopes where weathering is most active, and focus on areas with visible chalcedony outcrops as a sign of productive ground. Bring a small hand lens and 5-gallon bucket to stay within the 25 lb daily limit while maximizing specimen quality.
terrain_notes: Semi-arid sagebrush terrain at 5,500 to 6,500 feet elevation with scattered volcanic and sedimentary outcrops. Terrain is generally rolling to gently sloping with moderate foot traffic and uneven rocky footing. Most productive areas are within 1-3 miles of parking, accessible by hiking informal trails and washes. Vegetation includes low sage and scattered juniper. No wheelchair access; sturdy boots essential.
best_season: April - October. Spring and early summer offer reliable weather and easy accessibility, while fall (September-October) provides cooler temperatures ideal for long hikes. Winter storms can close roads and make high elevations inaccessible. Peak season is May through September.
written_directions: From Beaver, take US 89 south for approximately 12 miles. Turn east onto a marked BLM access road (watch for signs indicating public rockhounding areas). Follow the road for 2-3 miles to parking pullouts near the base of Rocky Range. Vehicles with good clearance are recommended as the access road may have washboard conditions. Multiple pullouts are available along the eastern flank of the range.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM access road with potential washboard conditions. Passable by passenger vehicles in dry conditions; high-clearance vehicles recommended after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Multiple unmarked pullouts along the access road near the base of Rocky Range. No formal parking area, but space available for 3-5 vehicles at each pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. BLM rockhounding is restricted to hand-collection methods. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand-collection methods only
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Beaver, Utah approximately 12-15 miles northwest. Gas, food, and water available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle terrain with rolling slopes and wash bottoms suitable for most hikers. No wheelchair access due to uneven rocky footing and informal trail conditions. Elevation gain is minimal (under 500 feet) on most productive slopes. Sturdy boots and sun protection essential.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Rocky Range near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: Rocky Range is best known for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The agate typically displays banding and fortification patterns, while jasper ranges from red to brown tones. Colorless to milky quartz is also common. Most specimens are marble to fist-sized, making them ideal for collectors of all skill levels.

faq.question: Is a permit required to rockhound at Rocky Range on BLM land in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Rocky Range. As BLM public land, it falls under FLPMA personal use collection rules. You may collect up to 25 lbs per day at no cost, making this a free rockhounding destination ideal for budget-conscious collectors.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find agate at Rocky Range, Utah?
faq.answer: The best season is April through October, with peak conditions in May through September when weather is most stable. Spring runoff in washes exposes fresh specimens, while fall offers cooler temperatures for extended hikes. Winter snow may close access roads, especially at higher elevations.

faq.question: How far is Rocky Range from Beaver, Utah and what vehicles can access it?
faq.answer: Rocky Range is approximately 14-15 miles south of Beaver via US 89 and a BLM access road. Passenger vehicles can access the main parking pullouts in dry conditions, though high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicles are recommended after rain due to washboard conditions on the unpaved road.

faq.question: Is Rocky Range suitable for kids and families to go rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Rocky Range is family and kid-friendly for ages 6 and up. Terrain is relatively gentle with minimal elevation gain, parking is close to productive areas, and specimens are accessible without specialized equipment. Children enjoy finding colorful agates on hillsides and in washes. Bring plenty of water and sun protection.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring when rockhounding at Rocky Range?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket to stay within the 25 lb daily limit, a hand lens for inspecting specimens, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and 1-2 gallons of water per person. A GPS device or offline map is recommended as cell service is spotty. No power tools or motorized equipment are allowed on this BLM land.

meta_title: Rocky Range Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Beaver, Utah for agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly public land collecting.
